answer:
Verdict: Criminal risk low; civil risk real and material. LinkedIn actively sues companies like yours and wins.

**Exposure by claim:**

1. **CFAA — low.** *hiQ v. LinkedIn* (9th Cir. 2022) + *Van Buren* (SCOTUS 2021): scraping truly public data isn't "unauthorized access." Caveat: if you use fake accounts, log in, or circumvent IP blocks/cease-and-desist, CFAA risk revives ("gates-up" theory).

2. **Breach of contract — high, this is what kills scrapers now.** On remand, hiQ *lost*: court held its scraping breached LinkedIn's User Agreement; hiQ settled, shut down. LinkedIn sued Proxycurl/Nubela (N.D. Cal., Jan 2025) on same theory — Proxycurl settled and exited the business. If you or your scraping vendors ever accepted LinkedIn's ToS (any employee account counts), you're exposed. Also: turnover of scraped data + fees is a typical settlement outcome.

3. **State privacy law — growing.** CCPA/CPRA applies: scraped data is personal info; you likely qualify as a "business" or "data broker." California's Delete Act requires data-broker registration; ~15 other state laws similar. FTC has pursued data brokers under Section 5.

4. **Secondary:** trespass to chattels, unjust enrichment, DMCA §1201 (if circumventing technical blocks), publicity-rights claims (profile photos/names used commercially — see *Facebook v. Power*, *IMDb* line; some class actions against scrapers-resellers).

**Practical risk profile:** LinkedIn sends cease-and-desist, then sues in N.D. Cal. Every commercial LinkedIn-scraping company that's fought them has settled or died (hiQ, Mantheos, Proxycurl). Your customers also face contamination risk — your enrichment data becomes toxic in their CRMs.

**Recommendations:**
- Don't scrape while logged in or via fake accounts — ever. That's the CFAA/criminal line.
- Audit whether your entity/employees/vendors are bound by LinkedIn ToS.
- Register as a data broker in CA/TX/OR/VT if you sell enriched personal data.
- Consider licensed alternatives (People Data Labs-style resellers shift, not eliminate, risk — check their sourcing warranties and indemnities).
- Budget for a C&D as the likely first event; have counsel lined up before it arrives.

Not legal advice; get a tech-litigation attorney to review your specific collection method — logged-in vs. logged-out is the whole ballgame.

Sources: [hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HiQ_Labs_v._LinkedIn) · [Privacy World: LinkedIn wins contract claim](https://www.privacyworld.blog/2022/11/federal-court-rules-in-favor-of-linkedins-breach-of-contract-claim-after-six-years-of-cfaa-data-scraping-litigation/) · [Nubela/Proxycurl account of 2025 LinkedIn suit](https://nubela.co/blog/is-scraping-linkedin-legal-in-2026/) · [Farella: rulings in hiQ and other scraping cases](https://www.fbm.com/publications/what-recent-rulings-in-hiq-v-linkedin-and-other-cases-say-about-the-legality-of-data-scraping/)
